ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249282, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249294, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249649, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9965d9c7e8f1', conversation_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9964ea94d796')

   - `legacy_customer_id__c` matches `id` exactly
   - `name` can be NULL (row 6)

2. **Contact**:
   - `id` is like `CON-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id (e.g., `CUST-1001`)
   - `role__c` has values like: `Technical Contact`, `Sponsor`, `Technischer Ansprechpartner`, `N/A`, `Entscheider`, ``
   - `preferred_language__c` has: `Französisch`, `Deutsch`, `French`, `Englisch`, `en`, `FR`
   - `lastname` is NOT NULL in target, but source has no NULLs in sample

3. **Opportunity**:
   - `id` is like `OPP-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id
   - `stagename` has: `closed won`, `In Prüfung`, `LOST`, `Prospecting`, `Prospect`, `Closed Lost`
   - `closedate` has formats: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`
   - `amount` has: `0`, `-383632.13`, `EUR 42543.61`, `60.702,05` (European format)
   - `currencyisocode` has: `Euro`, `Dollar`, `chf`, `USD`, `£`, `eur`

4. **Project__c**:
   - `id` is like `PROJ-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id
   - `opportunity__c` points to Opportunity.id
   - `project_status__c` has: `Aktiv`, `Planung`, `In Planung`, `completed`, `Storniert`, `Active`, `Completed`
   - `go_live_date__c` has: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `N/A`

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - `id` is like `AST-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id (but some have `_DUP` suffix like `CUST-1059_DUP`)
   - `project__c` points to Project__c.id
   - `warranty_end_date__c` has: `0000-00-00`, `YYYY-MM-DD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`

### Transformation Rules:
- **Enum mappings** need to be normalized
- **Dates** need parsing from multiple formats
- **Amounts** need currency symbol removal and European format handling
- **Legacy IDs** should come from source `id` columns
- **Account__c/AccountId** should reference the Salesforce-style Account `id`

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='Now I have a clear picture of the source data. Let me analyze the patterns and transformations needed:

### Key Observations:
1. **Account**:
   - `id` is like `CUST-1001` (Salesforce-style)
   - `customer_tier__c` has mixed case: `Platinum`, `GOLD`, `platinum`, `gold`, `BRONZE`, `SILBER`, `silver`, etc.
   - `region__c` has values like `DACH`, `UK`, `Nordics`, `Benelux`, `Southern Europe`
   - `legacy_customer_id__c` matches `id` exactly
   - `name` can be NULL (row 6)

2. **Contact**:
   - `id` is like `CON-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id (e.g., `CUST-1001`)
   - `role__c` has values like: `Technical Contact`, `Sponsor`, `Technischer Ansprechpartner`, `N/A`, `Entscheider`, ``
   - `preferred_language__c` has: `Französisch`, `Deutsch`, `French`, `Englisch`, `en`, `FR`
   - `lastname` is NOT NULL in target, but source has no NULLs in sample

3. **Opportunity**:
   - `id` is like `OPP-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id
   - `stagename` has: `closed won`, `In Prüfung`, `LOST`, `Prospecting`, `Prospect`, `Closed Lost`
   - `closedate` has formats: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`
   - `amount` has: `0`, `-383632.13`, `EUR 42543.61`, `60.702,05` (European format)
   - `currencyisocode` has: `Euro`, `Dollar`, `chf`, `USD`, `£`, `eur`

4. **Project__c**:
   - `id` is like `PROJ-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id
   - `opportunity__c` points to Opportunity.id
   - `project_status__c` has: `Aktiv`, `Planung`, `In Planung`, `completed`, `Storniert`, `Active`, `Completed`
   - `go_live_date__c` has: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `N/A`

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - `id` is like `AST-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id (but some have `_DUP` suffix like `CUST-1059_DUP`)
   - `project__c` points to Project__c.id
   - `warranty_end_date__c` has: `0000-00-00`, `YYYY-MM-DD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`

### Transformation Rules:
- **Enum mappings** need to be normalized
- **Dates** need parsing from multiple formats
- **Amounts** need currency symbol removal and European format handling
- **Legacy IDs** should come from source `id` columns
- **Account__c/AccountId** should reference the Salesforce-style Account `id`

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="The dbt project files have been created. Let me create a summary of what was produced:

## Summary of dbt Project Created

I've created a complete dbt project with the following files:

### 1. **models/sources.yml**
- Defines the source `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` pointing to the physical Postgres schema
- Declares all 5 source tables: `account`, `contact`, `opportunity`, `project__c`, `installed_asset__c`

### 2. **models/Account.sql**
- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account`
- Handles:
  - NULL name → 'Unknown'
  - Customer tier normalization (Platinum, Gold, Silver, Bronze) with case-insensitive matching including German variants
  - Legacy_Customer_ID__c from source `legacy_customer_id__c`
  - All other fields pass-through
  - CreatedDate/LastModifiedDate as current timestamp
  - IsDeleted = 0

### 3. **models/Contact.sql**
- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact`
- Handles:
  - NULL/empty lastname → 'Unknown'
  - Role__c normalization to enum values (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
  - Preferred_Language__c normalization to ISO codes (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
  - AccountId from source `accountid`
  - Legacy_Contact_ID__c from source `id`
  - Timeline fields and IsDeleted = 0

### 4. **models/Opportunity.sql**
- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity`
- Handles:
  - StageName normalization to all 10 enum values with case-insensitive matching including German variants
  - CloseDate parsing from multiple formats (YYYYMMDD, MM/DD/YYYY, DD.MM.YYYY, YYYY-MM-DD)
  - Amount parsing with:
    - Currency symbol removal (EUR, £, $, etc.)
    - European format handling (1.234,56 → 1234.56)
    - Negative values
    - Pure numeric values
  - CurrencyIsoCode normalization to standard ISO codes
  - AccountId from source `accountid`
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c from source `id`

### 5. **models/Project__c.sql**
- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c`
- Handles:
  - Project_Status__c normalization to enum values (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
  - Go_Live_Date__c parsing from multiple formats
  - Account__c from source `account__c`
  - Opportunity__c from source `opportunity__c`
  - Legacy_Project_ID__c from source `id`
  - Timeline fields and IsDeleted = 0

### 6. **models/Installed_Asset__c.sql**
- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c`
- Handles:
  - Warranty_End_Date__c parsing from multiple formats, with special handling for '0000-00-00' and 'N/A'
  - Account__c from source `account__c` (including _DUP variants)
  - Project__c from source `project__c`
  - Legacy_Asset_ID__c from source `id`
  - Timeline fields and IsDeleted = 0

All models:
- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}`
- Use `{{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', '<table>') }}` to reference sources
- Output column names match target spec exactly with double-quoted aliases for mixed-case
- Handle NULL/empty values appropriately
- Populate Legacy_*__c columns from source natural keys (id columns)
- Use AccountId/Account__c referencing Salesforce-style Account Ids
- Include CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, and IsDeleted fields

The project is now complete and ready to use."), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/sources.yml"}', tool_call_id='RmHYfmfzS')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=19997, output_tokens=927), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 56, 54, 982285, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 56, 12, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-a364e8b684235729', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249282, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:

- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL

- `project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:

- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account

## Opportunity

- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text

## Project__c

- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text

- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='Now I have a clear picture of the source data. Let me analyze the patterns and transformations needed:

### Key Observations:
1. **Account**:
   - `id` is like `CUST-1001` (Salesforce-style)
   - `customer_tier__c` has mixed case: `Platinum`, `GOLD`, `platinum`, `gold`, `BRONZE`, `SILBER`, `silver`, etc.
   - `region__c` has values like `DACH`, `UK`, `Nordics`, `Benelux`, `Southern Europe`
   - `legacy_customer_id__c` matches `id` exactly
   - `name` can be NULL (row 6)

2. **Contact**:
   - `id` is like `CON-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id (e.g., `CUST-1001`)
   - `role__c` has values like: `Technical Contact`, `Sponsor`, `Technischer Ansprechpartner`, `N/A`, `Entscheider`, ``
   - `preferred_language__c` has: `Französisch`, `Deutsch`, `French`, `Englisch`, `en`, `FR`
   - `lastname` is NOT NULL in target, but source has no NULLs in sample

3. **Opportunity**:
   - `id` is like `OPP-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id
   - `stagename` has: `closed won`, `In Prüfung`, `LOST`, `Prospecting`, `Prospect`, `Closed Lost`
   - `closedate` has formats: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`
   - `amount` has: `0`, `-383632.13`, `EUR 42543.61`, `60.702,05` (European format)
   - `currencyisocode` has: `Euro`, `Dollar`, `chf`, `USD`, `£`, `eur`

4. **Project__c**:
   - `id` is like `PROJ-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id
   - `opportunity__c` points to Opportunity.id
   - `project_status__c` has: `Aktiv`, `Planung`, `In Planung`, `completed`, `Storniert`, `Active`, `Completed`
   - `go_live_date__c` has: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `N/A`

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - `id` is like `AST-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id (but some have `_DUP` suffix like `CUST-1059_DUP`)
   - `project__c` points to Project__c.id
   - `warranty_end_date__c` has: `0000-00-00`, `YYYY-MM-DD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`

### Transformation Rules:
- **Enum mappings** need to be normalized
- **Dates** need parsing from multiple formats
- **Amounts** need currency symbol removal and European format handling

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249282, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL

- `industry` text NULL

- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

   - `legacy_customer_id__c` matches `id` exactly
   - `name` can be NULL (row 6)

2. **Contact**:
   - `id` is like `CON-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id (e.g., `CUST-1001`)
   - `role__c` has values like: `Technical Contact`, `Sponsor`, `Technischer Ansprechpartner`, `N/A`, `Entscheider`, ``
   - `preferred_language__c` has: `Französisch`, `Deutsch`, `French`, `Englisch`, `en`, `FR`
   - `lastname` is NOT NULL in target, but source has no NULLs in sample

3. **Opportunity**:
   - `id` is like `OPP-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id
   - `stagename` has: `closed won`, `In Prüfung`, `LOST`, `Prospecting`, `Prospect`, `Closed Lost`
   - `closedate` has formats: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`
   - `amount` has: `0`, `-383632.13`, `EUR 42543.61`, `60.702,05` (European format)
   - `currencyisocode` has: `Euro`, `Dollar`, `chf`, `USD`, `£`, `eur`

4. **Project__c**:
   - `id` is like `PROJ-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id
   - `opportunity__c` points to Opportunity.id
   - `project_status__c` has: `Aktiv`, `Planung`, `In Planung`, `completed`, `Storniert`, `Active`, `Completed`
   - `go_live_date__c` has: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `N/A`

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - `id` is like `AST-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id (but some have `_DUP` suffix like `CUST-1059_DUP`)
   - `project__c` points to Project__c.id
   - `warranty_end_date__c` has: `0000-00-00`, `YYYY-MM-DD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`

### Transformation Rules:
- **Enum mappings** need to be normalized
- **Dates** need parsing from multiple formats
- **Amounts** need currency symbol removal and European format handling
- **Legacy IDs** should come from source `id` columns
- **Account__c/AccountId** should reference the Salesforce-style Account `id`

### 1. **models/sources.yml**

- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account`
- Handles:
  - NULL name → 'Unknown'

### 3. **models/Contact.sql**
- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact`
- Handles:
  - NULL/empty lastname → 'Unknown'
  - Role__c normalization to enum values (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
  - Preferred_Language__c normalization to ISO codes (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
  - AccountId from source `accountid`
  - Legacy_Contact_ID__c from source `id`
  - Timeline fields and IsDeleted = 0

### 4. **models/Opportunity.sql**
- Materializes as a table
- Maps from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity`
- Handles:
  - StageName normalization to all 10 enum values with case-insensitive matching including German variants
  - CloseDate parsing from multiple formats (YYYYMMDD, MM/DD/YYYY, DD.MM.YYYY, YYYY-MM-DD)
  - Amount parsing with:
    - Currency symbol removal (EUR, £, $, etc.)
    - European format handling (1.234,56 → 1234.56)
    - Negative values
    - Pure numeric values
  - CurrencyIsoCode normalization to standard ISO codes
  - AccountId from source `accountid`
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c from source `id`

### 5. **models/Project__c.sql**
- Materializes as a table

- Handles:

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(name), ''), 'Untitled Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALIFICATION', 'QUALI', 'QUALIFIKATION', 'IN PRÜFUNG', 'IN KONTAKT') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'NEEDS ANALYSIS' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'VALUE PROPOSITION' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('ID. DECISION MAKERS', 'ID DECISION MAKERS') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'PERCEPTION ANALYSIS' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROPOSAL/PRICE QUOTE', 'PROPOSAL') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('NEGOTIATION/REVIEW', 'NEGOTIATION') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'WON', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)', 'GEWONNEN') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED LOST', 'LOST', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (VERLOREN)', 'VERLOREN') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN amount ~ '^[\d]+[.,\d]+$' THEN
            CASE
                WHEN amount ~ ',' THEN
                    CASE
                        WHEN amount ~ '\.' THEN
                            REGEXP_REPLACE(
                                REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '\.', '', 'g'),
                                ',',
                                '.',
                                'g'
                            )::DOUBLE PRECISION
                        ELSE
                            REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, ',', '.', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
                    END
                ELSE
                    amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
            END
        WHEN amount ~ '^[A-Za-z\s€£$]+[\d]+[.,\d]*$' THEN
            REGEXP_REPLACE(
                REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g'),
                ',',
                '.',
                'g'
            )::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN amount ~ '^[\d]+$' THEN amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN amount ~ '^-?[\d]+[.,\d]+$' THEN
            CASE
                WHEN amount ~ ',' THEN
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '\.', '', 'g'),
                        ',',
                        '.',
                        'g'
                    )::DOUBLE PRECISION
                ELSE
                    amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
            END
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR', '$', 'US DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF', 'SWISS FRANC', 'SFR') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', 'POUND', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE currencyisocode
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
', tool_call_id='J4TcmQpgP', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 57, 29, 232676, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 57, 29, 233112, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9965d9c7e8f1', conversation_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9964ea94d796')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249282, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:

## Opportunity
Columns:

- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249294, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249649, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9965d9c7e8f1', conversation_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9964ea94d796')

   - `legacy_customer_id__c` matches `id` exactly
   - `name` can be NULL (row 6)

2. **Contact**:
   - `id` is like `CON-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id (e.g., `CUST-1001`)
   - `role__c` has values like: `Technical Contact`, `Sponsor`, `Technischer Ansprechpartner`, `N/A`, `Entscheider`, ``
   - `preferred_language__c` has: `Französisch`, `Deutsch`, `French`, `Englisch`, `en`, `FR`
   - `lastname` is NOT NULL in target, but source has no NULLs in sample

3. **Opportunity**:
   - `id` is like `OPP-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id
   - `stagename` has: `closed won`, `In Prüfung`, `LOST`, `Prospecting`, `Prospect`, `Closed Lost`
   - `closedate` has formats: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`
   - `amount` has: `0`, `-383632.13`, `EUR 42543.61`, `60.702,05` (European format)
   - `currencyisocode` has: `Euro`, `Dollar`, `chf`, `USD`, `£`, `eur`

4. **Project__c**:
   - `id` is like `PROJ-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id
   - `opportunity__c` points to Opportunity.id
   - `project_status__c` has: `Aktiv`, `Planung`, `In Planung`, `completed`, `Storniert`, `Active`, `Completed`
   - `go_live_date__c` has: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `N/A`

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - `id` is like `AST-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id (but some have `_DUP` suffix like `CUST-1059_DUP`)
   - `project__c` points to Project__c.id

### Transformation Rules:
- **Enum mappings** need to be normalized

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(name), ''), 'Untitled Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALIFICATION', 'QUALI', 'QUALIFIKATION', 'IN PRÜFUNG', 'IN KONTAKT') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'NEEDS ANALYSIS' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'VALUE PROPOSITION' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('ID. DECISION MAKERS', 'ID DECISION MAKERS') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'PERCEPTION ANALYSIS' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROPOSAL/PRICE QUOTE', 'PROPOSAL') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('NEGOTIATION/REVIEW', 'NEGOTIATION') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'WON', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)', 'GEWONNEN') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED LOST', 'LOST', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (VERLOREN)', 'VERLOREN') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN amount ~ '^[\d]+[.,\d]+$' THEN
            CASE
                WHEN amount ~ ',' THEN
                    CASE
                        WHEN amount ~ '\.' THEN
                            REGEXP_REPLACE(
                                REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '\.', '', 'g'),
                                ',',
                                '.',
                                'g'
                            )::DOUBLE PRECISION
                        ELSE
                            REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, ',', '.', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
                    END
                ELSE
                    amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
            END
        WHEN amount ~ '^[A-Za-z\s€£$]+[\d]+[.,\d]*$' THEN
            REGEXP_REPLACE(
                REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g'),
                ',',
                '.',
                'g'
            )::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN amount ~ '^[\d]+$' THEN amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN amount ~ '^-?[\d]+[.,\d]+$' THEN
            CASE
                WHEN amount ~ ',' THEN
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '\.', '', 'g'),
                        ',',
                        '.',
                        'g'
                    )::DOUBLE PRECISION
                ELSE
                    amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
            END
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR', '$', 'US DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF', 'SWISS FRANC', 'SFR') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', 'POUND', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE currencyisocode
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
', tool_call_id='J4TcmQpgP', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 57, 29, 232676, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 57, 29, 233112, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9965d9c7e8f1', conversation_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9964ea94d796')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 25, 30, 249282, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text

- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text

## Contact
Columns:

- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='Now I have a clear picture of the source data. Let me analyze the patterns and transformations needed:

### Key Observations:
1. **Account**:
   - `id` is like `CUST-1001` (Salesforce-style)
   - `customer_tier__c` has mixed case: `Platinum`, `GOLD`, `platinum`, `gold`, `BRONZE`, `SILBER`, `silver`, etc.
   - `region__c` has values like `DACH`, `UK`, `Nordics`, `Benelux`, `Southern Europe`
   - `legacy_customer_id__c` matches `id` exactly
   - `name` can be NULL (row 6)

2. **Contact**:
   - `id` is like `CON-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id (e.g., `CUST-1001`)
   - `role__c` has values like: `Technical Contact`, `Sponsor`, `Technischer Ansprechpartner`, `N/A`, `Entscheider`, ``
   - `preferred_language__c` has: `Französisch`, `Deutsch`, `French`, `Englisch`, `en`, `FR`
   - `lastname` is NOT NULL in target, but source has no NULLs in sample

3. **Opportunity**:
   - `id` is like `OPP-00001`
   - `accountid` points to Account.id
   - `stagename` has: `closed won`, `In Prüfung`, `LOST`, `Prospecting`, `Prospect`, `Closed Lost`
   - `closedate` has formats: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`
   - `amount` has: `0`, `-383632.13`, `EUR 42543.61`, `60.702,05` (European format)
   - `currencyisocode` has: `Euro`, `Dollar`, `chf`, `USD`, `£`, `eur`

4. **Project__c**:
   - `id` is like `PROJ-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id
   - `opportunity__c` points to Opportunity.id
   - `project_status__c` has: `Aktiv`, `Planung`, `In Planung`, `completed`, `Storniert`, `Active`, `Completed`
   - `go_live_date__c` has: `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, `N/A`

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - `id` is like `AST-00001`
   - `account__c` points to Account.id (but some have `_DUP` suffix like `CUST-1059_DUP`)
   - `project__c` points to Project__c.id
   - `warranty_end_date__c` has: `0000-00-00`, `YYYY-MM-DD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`

### Transformation Rules:
- **Enum mappings** need to be normalized
- **Dates** need parsing from multiple formats
- **Amounts** need currency symbol removal and European format handling

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(name), ''), 'Untitled Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALIFICATION', 'QUALI', 'QUALIFIKATION', 'IN PRÜFUNG', 'IN KONTAKT') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'NEEDS ANALYSIS' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'VALUE PROPOSITION' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('ID. DECISION MAKERS', 'ID DECISION MAKERS') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'PERCEPTION ANALYSIS' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROPOSAL/PRICE QUOTE', 'PROPOSAL') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('NEGOTIATION/REVIEW', 'NEGOTIATION') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'WON', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)', 'GEWONNEN') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED LOST', 'LOST', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (VERLOREN)', 'VERLOREN') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN amount ~ '^[\d]+[.,\d]+$' THEN
            CASE
                WHEN amount ~ ',' THEN
                    CASE
                        WHEN amount ~ '\.' THEN
                            REGEXP_REPLACE(
                                REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '\.', '', 'g'),
                                ',',
                                '.',
                                'g'
                            )::DOUBLE PRECISION
                        ELSE
                            REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, ',', '.', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
                    END
                ELSE
                    amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
            END
        WHEN amount ~ '^[A-Za-z\s€£$]+[\d]+[.,\d]*$' THEN
            REGEXP_REPLACE(
                REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g'),
                ',',
                '.',
                'g'
            )::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN amount ~ '^[\d]+$' THEN amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN amount ~ '^-?[\d]+[.,\d]+$' THEN
            CASE
                WHEN amount ~ ',' THEN
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '\.', '', 'g'),
                        ',',
                        '.',
                        'g'
                    )::DOUBLE PRECISION
                ELSE
                    amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
            END
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR', '$', 'US DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF', 'SWISS FRANC', 'SFR') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', 'POUND', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE currencyisocode
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
', tool_call_id='J4TcmQpgP', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 57, 29, 232676, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 13, 11, 57, 29, 233112, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9965d9c7e8f1', conversation_id='019f5b39-9d07-7214-9ccf-9964ea94d796')
